nativeRange.habitat: Eutrophic, shallow, often temporary freshwater bodies such as ponds, ditches, rice paddies, and pools, where high concentrations of bacteria and phytoplankton support filter-feeding cladocerans.

decorPreferences: no copper or metal surfaces in contact with culture water

diet.notes: Moina is a filter feeder that has been documented consuming bacteria, single-celled algae, yeast, and fine organic particles suspended in the water. Common hobbyist culture foods include active dry yeast slurry, spirulina powder, green water (water rich in suspended algae), and boiled potato or carrot slices that encourage a bacterial bloom. It is generally advised to feed in small, frequent amounts rather than large doses, as overfeeding quickly fouls the culture water.

goodWith: monoculture only (kept as a feed-culture, not a display animal)
harvested and fed to fry of bettas, killifish, tetras, rainbowfish, livebearers, gouramis, and most egglayers
harvested for small adult nano fish (chili rasboras, Boraras, scarlet badis)
avoidWith: any predator in the culture container
copper- or zinc-containing decor, plumbing, or medications in contact with culture water
fish that are too large to benefit from sub-millimeter prey (will simply ignore them)

imageCandidates.notes: Ivan Sinkov, 2048x1536. Dark-field photo of a live Moina macrocopa showing the rounded translucent carapace, single dark compound eye and the diagnostic orange/yellow brood pouch full of developing embryos; whole animal in frame and sharp.

summary: Moina is a tiny freshwater crustacean, commonly called a water flea, widely cultured as live food for fish fry and small nano fish. Generally considered smaller and more prolific in culture than common Daphnia, it is particularly well-suited to newly hatched livebearer, killifish, and tetra fry.
careNotes: Moina is kept not as a display animal but as a cultured live food, so the husbandry below describes culture management rather than aquarium display care. Adult females typically reach roughly 0.5–1.5 mm, with reports of up to around 1.8 mm under optimal conditions; newborn neonates are around 0.2–0.4 mm and are small enough for most newly hatched livebearer, killifish, tetra, and rainbowfish fry that cannot yet take larger Daphnia. It is generally advised to culture at 22–30°C, with peak reproduction around 25–27°C; the species tolerates 4–35°C but reproduction slows dramatically below around 18°C and water quality tends to degrade quickly above 30°C. The reported tolerated pH range is 6.5–9.0, with an optimum around 7.5–8.0. Moina has been reported to be extremely sensitive to copper, zinc, chlorine, chloramine, pesticides, and most aquarium medications — dechlorinated tap water, RO water, spring water, or aged aquarium water is generally recommended for the culture, and copper-plumbed water or copper-containing decor should be avoided entirely. Most hobbyists culture Moina in small bare-bottom containers ranging from a 4–8 L jar or bucket up to around a 20 L tank; running three or four staggered backup cultures is widely recommended, because a single crash can eliminate the entire supply. Food options that have been reported as reliable include active dry yeast slurry, spirulina powder, green water (water rich in suspended algae), and bacterial blooms triggered by a small slice of boiled potato or carrot. Harvest by pouring the culture through a fine brine-shrimp net (around 150 microns) and rinsing the catch into the rearing tank.
breedingNotes: Moina reproduces extremely rapidly in culture. Under good conditions, populations are functionally all-female and reproduce by parthenogenesis — a form of asexual reproduction in which females produce offspring without fertilization — reaching sexual maturity in 4–7 days at 24–26°C. Each female has been reported to produce 4–22 neonates per brood, with new broods every 1–2 days, supporting population doubling times of roughly 2–5 days. Under deteriorating conditions (food shortage, overcrowding, temperature stress, or drying), the culture has been reported to switch to sexual reproduction and produce thick-shelled resting eggs called ephippia, which can survive desiccation for years and are the form in which the species is often shipped between hobbyists. To maintain a sustained supply, it is generally advised to keep multiple staggered cultures and refresh starter stocks every 2–4 weeks.

primary.name: The Shrimp Farm
primary.notes: Designated primary returned HTTP 403 Forbidden during this research run; substituted with aquariumbreeder.com (Shrimp and Snail Breeder) as functional primary. URL retained for reviewer reference.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping
additional.notes: Designated secondary returned HTTP 403 Forbidden during this research run; substituted with tropical-fish-keeping.com, copepodsforsale.com, and DRAK-Aquaristik for husbandry, and ResearchGate / Springer Hydrobiologia for body-size measurements. URL retained for reviewer reference.

additional.name: Aquariumbreeder (Shrimp and Snail Breeder) - Moina Profile and Culture Guide
additional.notes: Functional primary substitute. Source for taxonomy (family Moinidae), temperature optimum 21-29 C, pH 7-8.5, gH 3-10, culture container 4-8 L, lifespan 2-3 weeks (up to 70 days lab), brood size 4-22 every 1.5-2 days, extreme copper/zinc sensitivity.

additional.name: Tropical Fish Keeping - Moina (Moina macrocopa)
additional.notes: Cross-confirmed temperature 24-31 C ideal, pH 7.0-8.0, container 5-10 gal (~20-40 L), copper and chemical sensitivity, comparison to Daphnia pulex (smaller; higher protein/lower fat), open brood pouch.

additional.name: Copepodsforsale - Moina vs Daphnia
additional.notes: Cross-confirmed maturation at 4-5 days at 26 C, container 5-10 gal, NH3 < 0.2 mg/L, O2 > 3.5 mg/L, and that Moina is smaller than Daphnia magna (suitable for newborn fry).

additional.name: ResearchGate / Springer Hydrobiologia - Moina macrocopa body size data
additional.notes: Triangulation source for adult female body length: females reach ~1.65 mm typical, up to ~1.8 mm under high food at 25 C. Resolves a 2x size disagreement between aquariumbreeder (0.2-0.5 mm) and tropical-fish-keeping (0.5-1.0 mm).

crustacean.moltingNotes: Moina, like all cladocerans (small aquatic crustaceans that shed their outer shell as they grow), molts very frequently as part of normal growth and reproduction — generally reported at roughly every 1–2 days during active reproduction at 25°C, with each molt typically accompanying the release of a new brood from the brood pouch. Shed exoskeletons and accompanying neonates contribute fine particulates to the culture water, which is widely considered normal and not a sign of trouble.
crustacean.breedingNotes: Parthenogenetic under good conditions; produces ephippia (resting eggs) under stress. See top-level breedingNotes for full culture protocol.
